program represents the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") in [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football"). The Hawkeyes compete in the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"). Iowa joined the Conference (then known as the Western Conference or Big Nine) in 1899 and played their first Conference football season in 1900. They are a [Division I Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS) member of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA). The Hawkeyes play their home games in [Iowa City, Iowa](/wiki/Iowa_City,_Iowa "Iowa City, Iowa"), at [Kinnick Stadium](/wiki/Kinnick_Stadium "Kinnick Stadium"), with a capacity of 69,250. The Hawkeyes are coached by [Kirk Ferentz](/wiki/Kirk_Ferentz "Kirk Ferentz"), who is in his 27th season as the head coach and is the longest current tenured head coach in NCAA Division I FBS. The Hawkeyes have won 13 conference championships (including 11 in the Big Ten). Iowa has been ranked #1 in the AP and Coaches Poll 15 times.

The **Iowa Hawkeyes football** program represents the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") in [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football"). The Hawkeyes compete in the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"). Iowa joined the Conference (then known as the Western Conference or Big Nine) in 1899 and played their first Conference football season in 1900. They are a [Division I Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS) member of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA). The Hawkeyes play their home games in [Iowa City, Iowa](/wiki/Iowa_City,_Iowa "Iowa City, Iowa"), at [Kinnick Stadium](/wiki/Kinnick_Stadium "Kinnick Stadium"), with a capacity of 69,250. The Hawkeyes are coached by [Kirk Ferentz](/wiki/Kirk_Ferentz "Kirk Ferentz"), who is in his 27th season as the head coach and is the longest current tenured head coach in NCAA Division I FBS. The Hawkeyes have won 13 conference championships (including 11 in the Big Ten). Iowa has been ranked #1 in the AP and Coaches Poll 15 times.

## History of the team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History of the team")]

Main article: [History of Iowa Hawkeyes football](/wiki/History_of_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football "History of Iowa Hawkeyes football")

See also: [List of Iowa Hawkeyes football seasons](/wiki/List_of_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_seasons "List of Iowa Hawkeyes football seasons")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Iowa_vs._Nebraska_1916.jpg/250px-Iowa_vs._Nebraska_1916.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_vs._Nebraska_1916.jpg)

Iowa plays Nebraska on November 25, 1916.

Football was first played as a club sport at Iowa in 1872, with intramural games against other colleges played as early as 1882, but it was not until 1889 that the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") first officially recognized a varsity football team. Beginning in 1892, Iowa played for six seasons as a member of the [Western Interstate University Football Association](/wiki/Western_Interstate_University_Football_Association "Western Interstate University Football Association") against three other Midwestern flagship state universities. In 1899, again playing as an independent, Iowa completed its first undefeated football season, which led to an invitation to join the Western Conference, now known as the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"). In 1900, the Hawkeyes finished another undefeated season and won a share of the Western Conference title in their first year of conference play. In 1907, Iowa helped form the new [Missouri Valley Intercollegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Intercollegiate_Athletic_Association "Missouri Valley Intercollegiate Athletic Association") with the other three university members of the former WIUFA and participated in football in the new league, while retaining its existing membership in the Western Conference. Iowa tied for the league championship in the first season of competition, but would leave the conference soon thereafter to return to sole competition in the Western Conference.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Howard Jones era and 1920s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Howard Jones era and 1920s")]

Iowa claimed consecutive Big Ten titles in [1921](/wiki/1921_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1921 Iowa Hawkeyes football team") and [1922](/wiki/1922_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1922 Iowa Hawkeyes football team"), and were recognized as national champions in those years. The Hawkeyes won 20 straight games in the early 1920s under the guidance of [Hall of Fame](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame") coach [Howard Jones](/wiki/Howard_Jones_(American_football_coach) "Howard Jones (American football coach)"). But Jones soon left Iowa and established a powerhouse at [Southern California](/wiki/USC_Trojans_football "USC Trojans football"), and the Hawkeyes were abysmal for most of the 1930s.

### 1929 Big Ten suspension

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1929 Big Ten suspension")]

Iowa was expelled from the Big Ten on May 25, 1929. The reasons were officially unstated and university president Walter Jessup professed not to know why the faculty committee voted to expel the university. Suspicions of [player compensation](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association#Player_compensation_proposals "National Collegiate Athletic Association") and Iowa's inaction to address alleged ethics violations appear to have been the main cause.[[5]](#cite_note-ChicTrib1929-5) Following the 1929 season, the Big Ten faculty committee unanimously voted to reinstate Iowa to the conference on February 1, 1930. On December 11, 1929, Iowa had disqualified 27 players, presumably due to compensation issues, and was advised not to seek reinstatement of any of those players.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Eddie Anderson era (1939–1942, 1946–1949)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Eddie Anderson era (1939–1942, 1946–1949)")]

Little was expected of Iowa's 1939 team based upon its overall record in the decade, but led by their new coach [Eddie Anderson](/wiki/Eddie_Anderson_(American_football_coach) "Eddie Anderson (American football coach)") the team exceeded expectations and had a glorious run. Nicknamed the "Ironmen" because a small number of players shouldered the brunt of the time played, the [1939 Hawkeyes](/wiki/1939_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1939 Iowa Hawkeyes football team") scored several upset victories and vaulted into the national rankings. Though Iowa fell a game short of winning the Big Ten title, team MVP [Nile Kinnick](/wiki/Nile_Kinnick "Nile Kinnick") won almost every major national award, including the 1939 [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy").

### Forest Evashevski era (1952–1960)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Forest Evashevski era (1952–1960)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Forest_Evashevski.jpg/250px-Forest_Evashevski.jpg)](/wiki/File:Forest_Evashevski.jpg)

Coach [Forest Evashevski](/wiki/Forest_Evashevski "Forest Evashevski")

[Forest Evashevski](/wiki/Forest_Evashevski "Forest Evashevski") was hired as Iowa's head coach in 1952. He lured [Calvin Jones](/wiki/Cal_Jones "Cal Jones") to Iowa, where Jones became the first Hawkeye – and the first African-American – to win the [Outland Trophy](/wiki/Outland_Trophy "Outland Trophy") in 1955. From 1956 to 1960, Evashevski led Iowa to four finishes in the top five of the national rankings, three Big Ten Conference titles, two [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_Game "Rose Bowl Game") victories (in 1957 and 1959), and the 1958 [FWAA national championship](/wiki/Grantland_Rice_Trophy "Grantland Rice Trophy"). After the 1960 season, Evashevski left coaching to become Iowa's athletic director.

#### The Forgotten Season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The Forgotten Season")]

Main article: [1960 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/1960_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1960 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

In 1960 the Hawkeyes held on to the No. 1 ranking for much of the season. The Hawkeyes defeated No. 8 [Ohio State](/wiki/1960_Ohio_State_Buckeyes_football_team "1960 Ohio State Buckeyes football team"), No. 15 [Michigan State](/wiki/Michigan_State_Spartans_football "Michigan State Spartans football"), and No. 10 [Purdue](/wiki/Purdue_Boilermakers_football "Purdue Boilermakers football"). Iowa lost to rival No. 3 [Minnesota](/wiki/1960_Minnesota_Golden_Gophers_football_team "1960 Minnesota Golden Gophers football team"). The game was the only loss of the year for the [1960 Hawkeyes](/wiki/1960_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1960 Iowa Hawkeyes football team") and they shared the Big Ten title with Minnesota. However, at that time, the Big Ten did not allow their teams to go to any bowl except for the Rose Bowl. As such, Minnesota was picked over Iowa to go to Pasadena and Iowa was left out, despite a No. 2 ranking in the [Coaches' Poll](/wiki/Coaches%27_Poll "Coaches' Poll") and a No. 3 ranking in the AP. Minnesota went on to win the National Championship, as the final AP poll was conducted before their Rose Bowl loss to Washington. This season is known as the "Forgotten Season",[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] for despite ending the season with a No. 2/3 ranking and a share of the Big Ten title, the Hawks were left out of January play.

### 1960s and 70s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1960s and 70s")]

Evashevski's departure from the sidelines began a two-decade downturn in Iowa's fortunes. [Jerry Burns](/wiki/Jerry_Burns "Jerry Burns") coached from 1961 though 1965. He had a 16–27–2 record. His first team finished 5–4, which would be Iowa's last winning record for 20 years. [Ray Nagel](/wiki/Ray_Nagel "Ray Nagel") followed from 1966 to 1970 with a 16–32–2 record. A 3-3-2 record in 1963 and consecutive 5–5 records in 1968 and 1969 would be the Hawks' only non-losing records from 1962 to 1980. [Frank Lauterbur](/wiki/Frank_Lauterbur "Frank Lauterbur") followed, coaching from 1971 to 1973 with a 4–28–1 record, with a 0–11 record in 1973. [Bob Commings](/wiki/Bob_Commings "Bob Commings") coached the Hawkeyes from 1974 to 1978. His record was 18 wins and 37 losses.

### Hayden Fry era (1979–1998)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Hayden Fry era (1979–1998)")]

Hall of Fame coach [Hayden Fry](/wiki/Hayden_Fry "Hayden Fry") was hired after the 1978 season[[7]](#cite_note-FrysHire-7) to reverse Iowa's fortunes. Considering the awful state of the program upon his arrival, Fry didn't take long to return the Hawkeyes to respectability. In his third year, Fry led the Hawkeyes to their first winning season in 21 years, a share of the Big Ten title, and a berth in [the Rose Bowl](/wiki/1982_Rose_Bowl "1982 Rose Bowl")–only the third bowl appearance in school history. He had vowed to resign if he didn't get the Hawkeyes to a bowl within four years.

Fry would go on to lead the Hawkeyes to three Big Ten titles (one outright, two shared) and 14 bowl games (including two more Rose Bowls in 1986 and 1991). His best team was the 1985 unit, which won a then school-record 10 games and garnered Iowa's first outright Big Ten title in 27 years. That team spent most of October ranked No. 1 in both major polls, the highest they had been ranked that far into the season in two decades. Among the legacies that Fry left behind is the now iconic Tiger Hawk logo and a widely reviled pink visitors' locker room (better to "calm" the opponents). Fry retired in 1998, turning the program over to his former assistant [Kirk Ferentz](/wiki/Kirk_Ferentz "Kirk Ferentz").

### Kirk Ferentz era (1999–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Kirk Ferentz era (1999–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Iowa_Beats_Michigan_%2811034613444%29.jpg/250px-Iowa_Beats_Michigan_%2811034613444%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_Beats_Michigan_(11034613444).jpg)

Coach [Kirk Ferentz](/wiki/Kirk_Ferentz "Kirk Ferentz") talking to players during a 2013 game

Ferentz led Iowa to three consecutive top-10 finishes from 2002 to 2004 and two Big Ten titles. The Hawkeyes have been invited to 21 bowl games in the past 23 seasons, and in 35 bowl games over the last 42 seasons. Kirk Ferentz has been the head coach since Hayden Fry's retirement after the 1998 season, and has led the team to a number of Bowl appearances, including two Orange Bowls (2003, 2010) and the 2016 Rose Bowl. Iowa has cracked the Top 25 at the end of the season twelve times during the Kirk Ferentz era – No. 8 in 2002, 2003, and 2004, No. 20 in 2008, No. 7 in 2009, No. 9 in 2015, No. 25 in 2018, No. 15 in 2019, No.16 in 2020, No. 23 in 2021. No. 24 in 2023 and No. 17 in 2025. Under Ferentz, Iowa won the Big Ten West division three times (2015, 2021 & 2023).

### National championships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: National championships")]

Iowa has been selected [national champion](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") five times by NCAA-designated major selectors, in 1921, 1922, 1956, 1958, and 1960.[[8]](#cite_note-2018ncaabook-8): 111, 113 Iowa claims national championships for all five seasons.[[3]](#cite_note-storage.googleapis.com-3)

Iowa finished the 1958 regular season with a 7–1–1 record and a No. 2 ranking in the major wire-service polls—the [AP Poll](/wiki/AP_National_Championship_Trophy "AP National Championship Trophy") and the [Coaches' Poll](/wiki/AFCA_National_Championship_Trophy "AFCA National Championship Trophy")—and won the [1959 Rose Bowl](/wiki/1959_Rose_Bowl "1959 Rose Bowl") convincingly over No. 16 and 7–3 [California](/wiki/1958_California_Golden_Bears_football_team "1958 California Golden Bears football team"), 38–12, setting or tying six Rose Bowl records. The AP and Coaches' Polls had named 10–0 [Louisiana State](/wiki/1958_LSU_Tigers_football_team "1958 LSU Tigers football team") as national champions as well as 37 other selectors[[9]](#cite_note-redeag-9) The [Football Writers Association of America](/wiki/Football_Writers_Association_of_America "Football Writers Association of America") (FWAA) awarded the [Grantland Rice Trophy](/wiki/Grantland_Rice_Trophy "Grantland Rice Trophy") and their national championship to Iowa after the bowl games were played.[[10]](#cite_note-1958FWAAGrantlandRice-10)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Illinois

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Illinois")]

Main article: [Illinois–Iowa rivalry § Football](/wiki/Illinois%E2%80%93Iowa_rivalry#Football "Illinois–Iowa rivalry")

The series dates back to the first meeting in 1899 and has been played 79 times.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) When the Big Ten split into non-geographical "Leaders" and "Legends" divisions in 2011,[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Illini and Hawkeyes were placed in opposite divisions and weren't designated as protected annual cross-divisional so the series became intermittent again.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, in 2014, the conference scrapped that divisional format in favor of a more geographically friendly "East" and West" divisional arrangement.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In so doing, the Big Ten placed both Illinois and Iowa in the West division which revived the annual rivalry once again.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Perhaps the 1952 game is the most notable matchup in the history of the football rivalry.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Illinois won 33–13, but the game is more known for egregious Hawkeye penalties, angry Iowa fans throwing apples at Illini football players and a punch thrown between players on both teams that lead to the Big Ten suspending the series indefinitely.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The conference would not lift the indefinite suspension until 1967, the longest break in the history of the football rivalry.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Brandon_Scherff_hawkeyes.jpg/250px-Brandon_Scherff_hawkeyes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brandon_Scherff_hawkeyes.jpg)

[Brandon Scherff](/wiki/Brandon_Scherff "Brandon Scherff") carries off the [Cy-Hawk trophy](/wiki/Cy-Hawk_Trophy "Cy-Hawk Trophy") after a victory in [Ames](/wiki/Ames,_Iowa "Ames, Iowa") against [Iowa State](/wiki/Iowa_State_Cyclones_football "Iowa State Cyclones football").

### Iowa State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Iowa State")]

Main article: [Iowa–Iowa State football rivalry](/wiki/Iowa%E2%80%93Iowa_State_football_rivalry "Iowa–Iowa State football rivalry")

Iowa plays [Iowa State](/wiki/Iowa_State_Cyclones_football "Iowa State Cyclones football") annually for the Cy-Hawk Trophy, which began in 1977. The teams' first meeting was in 1894. The teams quit meeting after the 1934 season until they renewed their rivalry in 1977. It was in 1977 that the tradition of the Cy-Hawk Trophy began, when the Des Moines Athletic Club donated a trophy dedicated to the rivalry. It was quickly dubbed the "*Cy-Hawk Trophy*". Iowa leads the trophy series 31–17, and the overall series 47–25 through the 2025 season.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The game is currently held in early September near [Patriot Day](/wiki/Patriot_Day "Patriot Day").

### Minnesota

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Minnesota")]

Main article: [Iowa–Minnesota football rivalry](/wiki/Iowa%E2%80%93Minnesota_football_rivalry "Iowa–Minnesota football rivalry")

Iowa plays [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Golden_Gophers_football "Minnesota Golden Gophers football") annually for the [Floyd of Rosedale](/wiki/Floyd_of_Rosedale "Floyd of Rosedale"), which is Iowa's oldest trophy game that began in 1935. The teams' first meeting was in 1891.
Floyd of Rosedale was created in 1935 after Minnesota Governor [Floyd Olson](/wiki/Floyd_B._Olson "Floyd B. Olson") bet a prize pig that the Gophers would win the game, a goal which the Gophers would accomplish that year. After the death of the pig, its image was cast in bronze, and the Floyd of Rosedale trophy was created. Iowa leads the trophy series 46–43–2 while Minnesota leads the overall series 63–54–2 through the 2025 season.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Nebraska

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Nebraska")]

Main article: [Iowa–Nebraska football rivalry](/wiki/Iowa%E2%80%93Nebraska_football_rivalry "Iowa–Nebraska football rivalry")

Iowa plays [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska_Cornhuskers_football "Nebraska Cornhuskers football") annually for the Heroes Trophy, which began when Nebraska joined the Big Ten Conference in 2011. Since the inauguration of the series as a conference game, the game has been played on the [Friday](/wiki/American_football_on_Thanksgiving#College "American football on Thanksgiving") following Thanksgiving. Iowa leads the trophy series 11–4, while Nebraska leads the overall series 30–23–3 through the 2025 season.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Wisconsin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Wisconsin")]

Main article: [Iowa–Wisconsin football rivalry](/wiki/Iowa%E2%80%93Wisconsin_football_rivalry "Iowa–Wisconsin football rivalry")

Iowa has played [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin_Badgers_football "Wisconsin Badgers football") for the Heartland Trophy since 2004. The teams' first meeting was in 1894. The trophy series is tied 10–10 and Wisconsin leads the overall series 49–48–2 through the 2025 season.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b8/10sIowauniform.png)](/wiki/File:10sIowauniform.png)

1910s football uniform

Iowa's home jersey is black with white numerals, with gold and white stripes on the sleeves. The away jersey is white with black numerals, and gold stripes on the sleeves. Players' names are located above the numerals on the back of the jersey. Gold pants with a black stripe are worn with both the home and away jersey. Iowa's helmets are black with a black facemask. They also have a gold stripe and the gold Iowa Hawkeye logo included on both sides of the helmet.

In 1979, [Hayden Fry](/wiki/Hayden_Fry "Hayden Fry") helped to create the Tiger Hawk, the logo seen on Iowa's football helmets.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Since both teams shared the colors of black and yellow gold, Fry sought and gained permission from the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers"), the dominant [National Football League (NFL)](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") team of the 1970s, to overhaul Iowa's uniforms in the Steelers' image. Fry's idea was that if the team were going to act like winners, they first needed to dress like winners. Fry had originally asked Steelers defensive tackle ["Mean" Joe Greene](/wiki/Joe_Greene "Joe Greene") for a replica helmet and home jersey; Greene was able to send Fry to one of the team owners, and three days later, the owners sent Fry reproduction copies of the home and away uniform of Steelers quarterback [Terry Bradshaw](/wiki/Terry_Bradshaw "Terry Bradshaw"), making Iowa one of only a few schools to use the uniform scheme of an NFL team; others include the Blue Devils of [Duke University](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University") whose football uniforms resemble those of the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts"). Although the uniforms appear substantially the same, there are subtle differences, mainly in the font of the numerals, the scheme of the white away jerseys, and the width of the pants and jersey stripes.

The Hawkeyes have removed the Tiger Hawk helmet logo and the single yellow gold stripe from their game helmets on five occasions as a symbolic gesture of mourning. The first instance was on November 2, 1991, in recognition of the six victims of a [fatal campus shooting](/wiki/University_of_Iowa_shooting "University of Iowa shooting").[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30) The second occasion was for a December 29, 1996, appearance in the [Alamo Bowl](/wiki/1996_Alamo_Bowl "1996 Alamo Bowl"). It served to commemorate the family of linebacker Mark Mitchell, who were involved in a fatal vehicle accident while en route to the game. The accident resulted in the death of Mitchell's mother and severe injuries to his father and two brothers.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Third came on Veterans' Day 2011 when they used a red, white, and blue Tiger Hawk on one side and left the other side blank in honor of our fallen heroes against Michigan.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Fourth was September 12, 2015, when they honored [Tyler Sash](/wiki/Tyler_Sash "Tyler Sash"), former Iowa Safety and NFL Veteran, who had died on September 8, 2015.[[33]](#cite_note-33) To honor Sash, one Tiger Hawk was replaced with a large, gold "#9" a tribute to the jersey number Sash wore while he played at Iowa. The Tiger Hawk was removed for a fifth time during the [2019 Holiday Bowl](/wiki/2019_Holiday_Bowl "2019 Holiday Bowl") in San Diego, California to honor the passing of former Iowa Hawkeye football coach, Hayden Fry.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30) The Hawkeyes have won all five memorial games.

The Iowa Athletic Director has okayed only seven stickers on the helmets from 1985 to the present. The first was in 1985, when a gold disk appeared, with the black letters "*ANF*", which stands for America Needs Farmers. This sticker had remained in place until 1992 when the NCAA required teams to remove 'excessive' decals. However, it was brought back by head coach Kirk Ferentz in 2009 and has remained in place since. The second was a small black sticker on the back of the helmet, with white letters that spelled out "*EVY*", the nickname of legendary Iowa head coach, and athletic director, Forest Evashevski, to commemorate his death in 2009. The third was in memory of Iowa high school football coaching legend Ed Thomas, who was killed in his team's weight room by a former player. A small gold sticker with the black letters "*FFF*" placed near the crown of the helmet represents "Faith, Family, Football," a motto Coach Thomas preached to his players to represent what his players' priorities should be not only through the season, but throughout life. The fourth was a small green sticker, with the number 30 on it to honor former Hawkeye Safety [Brett Greenwood](/wiki/Brett_Greenwood "Brett Greenwood"), who had recently fallen into a coma while working out at his old high school. Fifth was a small black sticker with a gold "TS", worn on the back of the helmet to commemorate the death of former Hawkeye safety Tyler Sash. Another decal was commissioned to honor former Polk County deputy sheriff Ron Stewart died on October 9, 2016, at the age of 76. Stewart volunteered his time providing security for the Iowa Football team at home and road games from 1982 to 2014. For the remainder of the season, the Hawkeyes wore a decal on the back of their helmets bearing his initials "RS" within a sheriff's badge in his memory.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Most recently, a special helmet decal was worn to honor former Iowa Hawkeye football player Damon Bullock, who died at the age of 25 in March 2019. For the 2019 season opener, the Hawkeyes wore a gold circle with black lettering detailing "DB5" on the back of their helmets, a nod to Bullock's playing number from 2011 to 2014, in addition to a gold graduation cap, honoring the team's recent graduates.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Kinnick Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Kinnick Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Kinnick_east_grandstand_092218.jpg/250px-Kinnick_east_grandstand_092218.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kinnick_east_grandstand_092218.jpg)

Kinnick Stadium

Main article: [Kinnick Stadium](/wiki/Kinnick_Stadium "Kinnick Stadium")

Nile Kinnick Stadium, formerly known as *Iowa Stadium*, is the home stadium of the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") Hawkeyes in [Iowa City](/wiki/Iowa_City,_Iowa "Iowa City, Iowa"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"). It opened as Iowa Stadium in 1929; prior to that time, Iowa played its home games at [Iowa Field](/wiki/Iowa_Field "Iowa Field"). Iowa Stadium was renamed *Nile Kinnick Stadium* in 1972 in honor of [Nile Kinnick](/wiki/Nile_Kinnick "Nile Kinnick"), the 1939 [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") winner and the only Heisman winner in university history, who died in service during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It holds 69,250 people,[[36]](#cite_note-36) making it the 25th largest college football stadium in America and the 82nd largest sports stadium in the world.

## Notable games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Notable games")]

### 1953: No. 20 Iowa vs No. 1 Notre Dame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: 1953: No. 20 Iowa vs No. 1 Notre Dame")]

Main article: [1953 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/1953_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1953 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

On Nov. 21, 1953, an undefeated No. 1 ranked [Notre Dame team](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football") was set to face the Hawkeyes in South Bend. In the first quarter, Iowa defensive back Dusty Rice intercepted the Irish, which allowed the Hawkeyes to drive the ball 72 yards for an Iowa touchdown and a 7–0 lead. With only 2 seconds left in the first half, Irish tackle [Frank Varrichione](/wiki/Frank_Varrichione "Frank Varrichione") fell down with an injury which stopped the clock (Notre Dame had no time outs left). Varrichione went in that same play and the Irish scored on a touchdown pass to Dan Shannon, to tie the game 7–7 at the half. With the score still deadlocked late into the fourth quarter, Iowa intercepted a pass on their own 48-yard line. The Hawkeyes then scored on a touchdown pass to end [Frank Gilliam](/wiki/Frank_Gilliam_(American_football) "Frank Gilliam (American football)") to give the Hawkeyes a 14–7 lead with 2:06 left in the game, with Notre Dame having no time outs remaining. With only 6 seconds left and the clock ticking away Frank Varrichione came down with another injury which stopped the clock (again he went back in that very same play). Notre Dame then scored a touchdown to tie the game up and stay unbeaten. After the game, sportswriters such as [Grantland Rice](/wiki/Grantland_Rice "Grantland Rice") and others were infuriated calling it unfair, and the Irish earned the label: "The Fainting Irish of Notre Dame". Iowa head coach Forest Evashevski said after the game: "When the One Great Scorer comes to write against our name, He won't write whether we won or lost, but how come we got gypped at Notre Dame". The Hawkeyes, who were ranked No. 20 before the game, jumped teams with better records to gain the No. 9 ranking. Varrichione has since admitted that the injuries were fake, in Steve Delsohn's book, *Talking Irish: The Oral History Of Notre Dame Football*. In addition, Notre Dame Heisman winner [Johnny Lattner](/wiki/Johnny_Lattner "Johnny Lattner") praised his team's tactics calling it "Pretty smart thinking, wasn't it?".[[37]](#cite_note-37) Because of this game, the NCAA changed the rules making players sit out at least one down before returning to the game.

### 1956: No. 6 Ohio State at No. 7 Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: 1956: No. 6 Ohio State at No. 7 Iowa")]

Main article: [1956 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/1956_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1956 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

On November 17, Iowa (ranked No. 7 in the AP Poll) defeated Ohio State (ranked No. 6), 6–0, before a crowd of 57,732 at Iowa Stadium. Ohio State went into the game with the second best rushing attack in the country but were held to 147 rushing yards, their lowest rushing yardage total in two years. The result broke Ohio State's winning streak of 17 games against conference opponents and clinched for Iowa the conference championship and a berth in the Rose Bowl. After time expired, Iowa fans hauled down the goal posts and paraded through Iowa City.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### 1957 Rose Bowl: No. 3 Iowa vs. No. 10 Oregon State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: 1957 Rose Bowl: No. 3 Iowa vs. No. 10 Oregon State")]

Main article: [1957 Rose Bowl](/wiki/1957_Rose_Bowl "1957 Rose Bowl")

Aided by three rushing touchdowns, the Big Ten Champion Iowa Hawkeyes got off to a 21–6 start at the half. After the half, Iowa scored two more touchdowns and beat Oregon State 35–19. With the victory, Iowa won their first Rose Bowl Game in program history. Quarterback [Ken Ploen](/wiki/Ken_Ploen "Ken Ploen") was the game's MVP ending the game with one rushing touchdown and one passing touchdown.

### 1959 Rose Bowl: No. 2 Iowa vs. No. 16 California

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: 1959 Rose Bowl: No. 2 Iowa vs. No. 16 California")]

Main article: [1959 Rose Bowl](/wiki/1959_Rose_Bowl "1959 Rose Bowl")

This game was lopsided and by the end of the third quarter, with a 22–6 advantage over the [California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears_football "California Golden Bears football"), Iowa could smell victory (eventually winning by a final margin of 38–12). With their win in the Rose Bowl in 1959, the No. 2 Iowa Hawkeyes (8–1–1) won the [Grantland Rice Trophy](/wiki/Grantland_Rice_Trophy "Grantland Rice Trophy") (in recognition of the national champion) as awarded by the [Football Writers Association of America](/wiki/Football_Writers_Association_of_America "Football Writers Association of America") (FWAA) following the bowl games.

No. 1 [Louisiana State](/wiki/1958_LSU_Tigers_football_team "1958 LSU Tigers football team") (11–0) was crowned [national champions](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") in both major polls, [AP](/wiki/AP_National_Championship_Trophy "AP National Championship Trophy") and [Coaches'](/wiki/AFCA_National_Championship_Trophy "AFCA National Championship Trophy"), before the bowl games were played. The LSU Tigers went on to claim a shutout victory in the [Sugar Bowl](/wiki/1959_Sugar_Bowl "1959 Sugar Bowl") over No. 12 Clemson.

### 1981: No. 7 Nebraska at Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 1981: No. 7 Nebraska at Iowa")]

Main article: [1981 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/1981_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1981 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

Official recap—There weren't many among the 60,160 who jammed Iowa's Kinnick Stadium who thought they were watching a matchup of the eventual Midwest entries in the Rose and Orange Bowls. There probably weren't a whole lot more who thought Iowa would avenge a 57–0 slaughter the Hawkeyes had suffered in Lincoln the year before. However, Iowa took advantage of excellent field position to jump to a 10–0 first-half lead, then held on to win a 10–7 stunner over the seventh-ranked Huskers.

The Hawkeye defense held the Huskers to their lowest total offense output of the season—234 yards—and stopped the Nebraska offense three straight times in Iowa territory after Roger Craig's one-yard TD had pulled the Huskers within 10–7 early in the final period. NU was stymied in the fourth quarter by a missed 30-yard field goal, a fumble, and Lou King's diving interception, which secured Iowa's victory with 39 seconds remaining.

In the first half, the Hawkeyes capitalized on a short punt and an interception to set up a two-yard TD run by Eddie Phillips and a 35-yard field goal by Lon Olejniczak. Meanwhile, the Huskers were held scoreless through three quarters for the first time since 1973 and didn't get out of their own territory until late in the second period, partly because of Iowa punter Reggie Roby's 50-plus yard average. NU quarterbacks Mark Maurer and Nate Mason completed only 8-of-18 passes for 81 yards and rushed 12 times for minus-2 yards.

Iowa used its upset win as a launching pad to defeat such teams as UCLA, Michigan and Purdue and post its first winning season since 1961.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

"At this minute, this is the greatest victory of my life," Iowa coach Hayden Fry said. "I'm getting to be an old man and I don't want to hurt any of my other teams that pulled off some upsets. But while I've been at Iowa, this is far and away the greatest victory.

"If you stay with this game long enough, the worm is bound to turn. Don't think we didn't earn this one, either. We did it fair and square, plus we showed a lot of character."[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### 1985: No. 2 Michigan at No. 1 Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: 1985: No. 2 Michigan at No. 1 Iowa")]

Main article: [1985 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/1985_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "1985 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

The Hawkeyes trailed 10–9 late in the fourth quarter, in what may be considered the greatest game played at [Kinnick Stadium](/wiki/Kinnick_Stadium "Kinnick Stadium"). Iowa got the ball, with 5:27 left, on their own 22-yard line. Led by their All-American Quarterback, [Chuck Long](/wiki/Chuck_Long "Chuck Long"), Iowa drove the ball to the [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football") 12-yard line. As the clock expired, kicker [Rob Houghtlin](/wiki/Rob_Houghtlin "Rob Houghtlin") sent one through the uprights, to give Iowa the win. The Hawkeyes would go on to accept an invitation to the [1986 Rose Bowl](/wiki/1986_Rose_Bowl "1986 Rose Bowl").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### 2005 Capital One Bowl: No. 11 Iowa vs. No. 12 LSU ("The Catch")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: 2005 Capital One Bowl: No. 11 Iowa vs. No. 12 LSU (\"The Catch\")")]

Main article: [2005 Capital One Bowl](/wiki/2005_Capital_One_Bowl "2005 Capital One Bowl")

The game has gone down in Hawkeye history known simply as "The Catch". Iowa was set to play the defending National Champion [LSU Tigers](/wiki/LSU_Tigers_football "LSU Tigers football"). Despite leading the entire game, Iowa found itself down 24–25 and got the ball with only 46 seconds left on the clock. With only a few seconds left in the game, Iowa found itself on their own 44-yard line facing a 2nd and 6. Iowa Quarterback [Drew Tate](/wiki/Drew_Tate "Drew Tate") threw the ball 56 yards to Warren Holloway for an Iowa touchdown that gave the Hawkeyes a 30–25 victory over [Nick Saban](/wiki/Nick_Saban "Nick Saban")'s defending champion Tigers. The touchdown was Holloway's first and only career touchdown.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### 2008: No. 3 Penn State at Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: 2008: No. 3 Penn State at Iowa")]

Main article: [2008 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/2008_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "2008 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

The undefeated [Penn State Nittany Lions](/wiki/2008_Penn_State_Nittany_Lions_football_team "2008 Penn State Nittany Lions football team") came into the game ranked No. 3 in the country with hopes of a BCS national championship. The Hawkeyes, on the other hand, were 5–4 and coming off a tough loss at [Illinois](/wiki/2008_Illinois_Fighting_Illini_football_team "2008 Illinois Fighting Illini football team"). The Hawkeyes jumped out to an early lead following a Penn State sack-fumble on 3rd down during its first drive, but the Nittany Lions would come back to take a 13–7 lead into halftime. With less than four minutes left in the game, and trailing 23–21, Iowa strong safety [Tyler Sash](/wiki/Tyler_Sash "Tyler Sash") intercepted Penn State quarterback [Daryll Clark](/wiki/Daryll_Clark "Daryll Clark")'s pass inside Iowa territory. The Hawkeyes drove to within field goal range where kicker Daniel Murray made a 31-yard field goal with :01 left to play. Iowa would go on to win 24–23 and hand Penn State its only regular season loss of the year. The Hawkeyes would go on to beat [South Carolina](/wiki/2008_South_Carolina_Gamecocks_football_team "2008 South Carolina Gamecocks football team") in the [Outback Bowl](/wiki/2009_Outback_Bowl "2009 Outback Bowl"). The win would be the first in a 13-game winning streak for Iowa stretching into the [2009 season](/wiki/2009_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "2009 Iowa Hawkeyes football team").

### 2010 Orange Bowl: No. 9 Georgia Tech vs. No. 10 Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: 2010 Orange Bowl: No. 9 Georgia Tech vs. No. 10 Iowa")]

Main article: [2010 Orange Bowl](/wiki/2010_Orange_Bowl "2010 Orange Bowl")

The game pitted the ACC Champion Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets against No. 10 Iowa. On Iowa's second drive of the game, quarterback [Ricky Stanzi](/wiki/Ricky_Stanzi "Ricky Stanzi") drove the Hawkeyes 80 yards, culminating in a 3-yard touchdown pass to [Marvin McNutt](/wiki/Marvin_McNutt "Marvin McNutt"), putting Iowa on top 7–0. Iowa scored another touchdown on their next drive, following a Georgia Tech punt, with Stanzi hitting Colin Sandeman for 21 yards, extending the Hawkeyes lead to 14–0. On the ensuing drive, GT punted for the third time in a row. On Iowa's next drive, Stanzi was intercepted by Jerrard Tarrant who returned the pick for a touchdown, cutting Iowa's lead to 14–7. Georgia Tech received the ball at the start of the second half. Iowa took over on downs after Georgia Tech missed a 41-yard field goal. On Iowa's next drive, they extended their lead by three thanks to a 33-yard field goal from kicker Daniel Murray. At the start of the 4th Quarter, Iowa had a 17–7 lead. This lead was cut when Georgia Tech drove down the field for its first and only offensive touchdown of the game. The touchdown cut Iowa's lead to 17–14. However, Iowa running back Brandon Wegher sealed the win for the Hawkeyes with his 32-yard rushing touchdown late in the 4th Quarter. Iowa beat Georgia Tech by a final score of 24–14.

The win marked the first BCS Bowl win for the Iowa program (Iowa had won two Rose Bowls prior to the formation of the BCS). The win also marked Iowa's first Orange Bowl victory. Iowa is one of only five Big Ten teams to compete in and win an Orange Bowl.

### 2016: No. 2 Michigan at Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: 2016: No. 2 Michigan at Iowa")]

Main article: [2016 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/2016_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "2016 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/PAB_FB_Iowa2016_72_%2830895510701%29.jpg/330px-PAB_FB_Iowa2016_72_%2830895510701%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:PAB_FB_Iowa2016_72_(30895510701).jpg)

[Manny Rugamba](/wiki/Emmanuel_Rugamba "Emmanuel Rugamba") intercepts a Michigan pass at Kinnick Stadium in 2016.

Iowa entered this matchup against the [9–0 Wolverines](/wiki/2016_Michigan_Wolverines_football_team "2016 Michigan Wolverines football team") with a 5–4 record, a letdown given the high expectations entering the season. Michigan arrived in Iowa City with the No. 1 scoring defense and the No. 3 scoring offense in the country. A week after being humbled 41–14 at [Penn State](/wiki/2016_Penn_State_Nittany_Lions_football_team "2016 Penn State Nittany Lions football team"), in the midst of a three-game home losing streak, and after trailing 10–0 in the second quarter of this game, the Hawkeyes put forth a gritty, spirited effort. Freshman [Keith Duncan](/wiki/Keith_Duncan "Keith Duncan") drilled a 33-yard field goal as time expired to deliver Iowa a much needed win. [Akrum Wadley](/wiki/Akrum_Wadley "Akrum Wadley") was the offensive standout for the Hawkeyes with 115 yards rushing, and another 52 yards receiving that included a touchdown late in the first half. The Hawkeye defense did their part as well, allowing only 201 total yards and scoring a safety in the 14–13 win.

With Clemson and Washington both losing, this marked only the second time No. 2, No. 3, and No. 4 fell during the same day in the regular-season. The other time was October 19, 1985, and one of the games that day was No. 1 Iowa's 12–10 win over No. 2 Michigan.

### 2017: No. 3 Ohio State at Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: 2017: No. 3 Ohio State at Iowa")]

Main article: [2017 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/2017_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "2017 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

Iowa entered this matchup against the [7–1 Buckeyes](/wiki/2017_Ohio_State_Buckeyes_football_team "2017 Ohio State Buckeyes football team") with a 5–3 record. At kickoff, the Buckeyes were ranked No. 6 in the nation and although the game was being played at Iowa's Kinnick Stadium, Ohio State was a 21-point favorite to win. Early on, it was an even contest as the teams traded scores on their way to a 17–17 midway through the second quarter. However, in the last 3 minutes of the first half, Iowa TE Noah Fant was on the receiving end of two Nate Stanley TD passes, sending Iowa into the locker room with a 31–17 lead. The second half belonged to the Hawkeyes as they sliced through the Ohio State defense on TD drives of 78, 60 and 47 yards on their way to a resounding 55–24 win.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Notable performances from the game were five TD passes by Iowa QB Nathan Stanley, Iowa CB Joshua Jackson's 3 interceptions, Iowa RB Akrum Wadley rushing for 118 yards on 20 carries (5.9) and Ohio State QB [J. T. Barrett](/wiki/J._T._Barrett "J. T. Barrett") throwing four INT's along with three TD's. On the day Iowa racked up a total of 487 yards against an Ohio State defense that came into the game only allowing 302 yards per contest. Many pundits remarked after the season that the lop-sided loss to Iowa was the reason that Ohio State was excluded from the College Football Playoff, even though the Buckeyes had won the Big Ten championship.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### 2019: No. 8 Minnesota at No. 20 Iowa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: 2019: No. 8 Minnesota at No. 20 Iowa")]

Main article: [2019 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/2019_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "2019 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

The Hawkeyes entered this game following a close road loss (24–22) at [Wisconsin](/wiki/2019_Wisconsin_Badgers_football_team "2019 Wisconsin Badgers football team"). Minnesota, on the other hand, was coming in with an undefeated 9–0 record and just off a home win against then-undefeated Penn State.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

However, the Gophers' undefeated season would end during this match, as they fell 23–19 to the Hawkeyes, despite a late comeback rally. Iowa kept the Gophers outside the end zone for two quarters, forcing two Gopher field goals. At the end of the half, the Hawkeyes were up 20–6. The Gophers would respond mightily in the second half, both on offense and defense, outscoring their opponent 13–3 with two touchdowns, one in the third and one in the fourth quarter. They forced a turnover late in the fourth quarter with a chance to win the game, but back to back sacks on first and second down sidelined quarterback [Tanner Morgan](/wiki/Tanner_Morgan "Tanner Morgan") with an injury, and Cole Kramer had to step in on a 3rd down and 21. His hail mary attempt was ultimately intercepted by [Riley Moss](/wiki/Riley_Moss "Riley Moss"), clinching the Hawkeyes' upset.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Minnesota would clinch the Big Ten West and play [Ohio State](/wiki/2019_Ohio_State_Buckeyes_football_team "2019 Ohio State Buckeyes football team") in the Big Ten Championship if they won this match, even if they were to later fall to Wisconsin in the season finale, since the Badgers had two conference losses. However, this loss opened the door for Wisconsin to regain a shot at winning the West, which they were able to pull off by upsetting the Gophers on the road.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

This was also the first of four straight wins for Iowa to end the season. Many pundits called them the "strongest 3-loss team in the country" prior to the game, as they lost in three road games by a total of 14 points to conference powers Michigan, Penn State and Wisconsin.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Since 2008, Iowa is 5–1 against AP-top 10 teams at home, their lone loss coming in 2017 to Penn State on a touchdown pass as the clock expired.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### 2021: No. 3 Iowa vs No. 4 Penn State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: 2021: No. 3 Iowa vs No. 4 Penn State")]

Main article: [2021 Iowa Hawkeyes football team](/wiki/2021_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "2021 Iowa Hawkeyes football team")

Extending from the previous season, Penn State was on a 10-game winning streak and Iowa was on a 12-game winning streak. Both teams were ranked in the Top 5 and [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")'s [Big Noon Kickoff](/wiki/Big_Noon_Kickoff "Big Noon Kickoff") came to Iowa City for the game.

Penn State jumped out to an early 17–3 lead before Iowa closed the gap to 7 by the end of the half. In the 3rd Quarter Penn State would extend the lead to 20–10, but the Nittany Lions would not score again in the game. Iowa would again close the gap to 7 points with a field goal to close out scoring in the 3rd Quarter. Iowa scored another field goal and a touchdown in the 4th Quarter which was enough to give Iowa the 23–20 win.[[51]](#cite_note-51) After the game, Iowa extended its season record to 6–0 and obtained its third win over a ranked team in the early season. As a result of Alabama's loss to Texas A&M, Iowa would go on to be ranked #2 in the AP and Coaches Poll the following week.

## Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Traditions")]

### Songs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Songs")]

Iowa's official fight song is the "[Iowa Fight Song](/wiki/Iowa_Fight_Song "Iowa Fight Song")" which is sung by the marching band and the fans. Iowa's school song is "[On Iowa](/wiki/On_Iowa "On Iowa")". Iowa also plays a third fight song, entitled "[Roll Along Iowa](/wiki/Roll_Along_Iowa "Roll Along Iowa")". After victories the band plays the [Im Himmel gibt's kein Bier](/wiki/Im_Himmel_gibt%27s_kein_Bier "Im Himmel gibt's kein Bier") Polka, which translates to "In Heaven There is No Beer". Before the game (since 2005) the team has exited the tunnel together to "[Back in Black](/wiki/Back_in_Black_(song) "Back in Black (song)")" by [AC/DC](/wiki/AC/DC "AC/DC") before joining hands and running onto the field in unison to "[Enter Sandman](/wiki/Enter_Sandman "Enter Sandman")" by [Metallica](/wiki/Metallica "Metallica").[[52]](#cite_note-52) This became known as "The Swarm."

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Mascot")]

Iowa's mascot is [Herky the Hawk](/wiki/Herky_the_Hawk "Herky the Hawk"), a black and gold caricature of a Hawk. Herky was created as a cartoon in 1948, and first appeared at a sporting event in 1959. Herky was actually named after the Greek God Hercules. The term "Hawkeye" originally appeared in the 19th century historical novel [The Last of the Mohicans](/wiki/The_Last_of_the_Mohicans "The Last of the Mohicans") by [James Fenimore Cooper](/wiki/James_Fenimore_Cooper "James Fenimore Cooper"); it was later used in its plural form to describe the people of the state of Iowa. The University of Iowa adopted this as the nickname for its athletic teams.

### Hawkeye Marching Band

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Hawkeye Marching Band")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Hawkeye_Marching_Band.jpg/250px-Hawkeye_Marching_Band.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hawkeye_Marching_Band.jpg)

[Hawkeye Marching Band](/wiki/Hawkeye_Marching_Band "Hawkeye Marching Band")

Founded in 1881, the [Hawkeye Marching Band](/wiki/Hawkeye_Marching_Band "Hawkeye Marching Band") now performs at all Iowa Hawkeye home football games. The band also travels with the team to usually one away game per year and any post-season bowl games.

### Kinnick Wave

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Kinnick Wave")]

In [2017](/wiki/2017_Iowa_Hawkeyes_football_team "2017 Iowa Hawkeyes football team"), Hawkeyes fans created what [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") called "college football's coolest new tradition". In February of that year, the university's [children's hospital](/wiki/University_of_Iowa_Children%27s_Hospital "University of Iowa Children's Hospital"), which has long had a close relationship with the Iowa football program, opened a new 12-story building across the street from Kinnick Stadium, with the top few floors featuring an unobstructed view of the playing field.[[53]](#cite_note-Sherman-53) The top floor of the hospital features the Press Box Café, a lounge area with floor-to-ceiling windows facing the field that is reserved for patients and their families on Hawkeyes game days, with big-screen TVs available when Iowa plays on the road.[[53]](#cite_note-Sherman-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Bain-54) Following a suggestion posted to a Hawkeyes fan page on [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), by Iowan Krista Young, fans now face the hospital and wave to the children and their families at the end of the first quarter of every home game. For home night games, the first of which was against [Penn State](/wiki/2017_Penn_State_Nittany_Lions_football_team "2017 Penn State Nittany Lions football team") on September 23, 2017, "The Wave" features fans waving with their [cell phone](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") flashlights turned on.[[54]](#cite_note-Bain-54) Two weeks after the Penn State game, with the Hawkeyes at home against [Illinois](/wiki/2017_Illinois_Fighting_Illini_football_team "2017 Illinois Fighting Illini football team"), the Hawkeye Marching Band joined in this new tradition, forming a hand moving side-to-side and "waving" at the children during its halftime show.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The Wave was even seen in the offseason—on February 15, 2018, inspired by a suggestion from nurses at the children's hospital, the stadium's maintenance crew created a pattern of a waving hand in snow that was covering the playing field. At least one patient was reported to have seen the crew creating the design, and upon completion, the crew went into the Press Box Café to see their final product.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The tradition has even spread beyond the Iowa fan base. With Iowa playing at [Michigan State](/wiki/2017_Michigan_State_Spartans_football_team "2017 Michigan State Spartans football team") on September 30, ESPN's *[College GameDay](/wiki/College_GameDay_(football) "College GameDay (football)")* aired a six-minute feature on The Wave, followed by the feature's reporter, [Tom Rinaldi](/wiki/Tom_Rinaldi "Tom Rinaldi"), leading the crowd at the [Virginia Tech](/wiki/Virginia_Tech "Virginia Tech") campus in a wave of their own.[[57]](#cite_note-57) A few hours later at the end of the first quarter of the Iowa–Michigan State game, Spartans fans joined in The Wave with visiting Iowa fans.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Disney Sports announced it would present the Iowa football program with its [Disney's Wide World of Sports Spirit Award](/wiki/Disney%27s_Wide_World_of_Sports_Spirit_Award "Disney's Wide World of Sports Spirit Award"), which annually goes to college football's most inspirational figure on November 20, 2017. The award was presented to the University of Iowa for The Wave on ESPN on December 7, 2017.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Retired numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Retired numbers")]

See also: [List of NCAA football retired numbers](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_football_retired_numbers "List of NCAA football retired numbers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Nile_Kinnick.jpg/250px-Nile_Kinnick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nile_Kinnick.jpg)

HB [Nile Kinnick](/wiki/Nile_Kinnick "Nile Kinnick"), 1939 [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") winner and one of the two numbers retired by Iowa

| **Iowa Hawkeyes retired numbers** | | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| No. | Player | Pos. | Tenure | Ref. |
| **24** | [Nile Kinnick](/wiki/Nile_Kinnick "Nile Kinnick") | [HB](/wiki/Halfback_(American_football) "Halfback (American football)") | 1936–1939 | [[60]](#cite_note-iow-60) |
| **62** | [Cal Jones](/wiki/Cal_Jones "Cal Jones") | [G](/wiki/Guard_(gridiron_football) "Guard (gridiron football)") | 1952–1955 | [[60]](#cite_note-iow-60) |

Two numbers have been retired by the Hawkeye football program, [Nile Kinnick](/wiki/Nile_Kinnick "Nile Kinnick")'s No. 24 and [Cal Jones](/wiki/Cal_Jones "Cal Jones")' No. 62. Both Kinnick and Jones were [consensus first team All-Americans](/wiki/College_Football_All-America_Team "College Football All-America Team"), and both men died in separate plane crashes before their 25th birthday.

Kinnick won the University of Iowa's only [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") in 1939 and is the man for whom [Kinnick Stadium](/wiki/Kinnick_Stadium "Kinnick Stadium") is named.

Jones was the first African-American to win the [Outland Trophy](/wiki/Outland_Trophy "Outland Trophy") and is one of only two Hawkeyes to be named Consensus [All-American](/wiki/College_Football_All-America_Team "College Football All-America Team") two times. Jones also finished 10th in the Heisman Trophy voting, an impressive finish for a lineman.

## Iowa and the NFL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Iowa and the NFL")]

### First round NFL draft picks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iowa_Hawkeyes_football&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: First round NFL draft picks")]

Iowa has had at least one player drafted in every [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") since 1978. Through the [2020 NFL draft](/wiki/2020_NFL_draft "2020 NFL draft"), Iowa has had 294 draft picks. 254 in the [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), 21 in the [AFL](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League"), and 7 in the [AAFC](/wiki/All-America_Football_Conference "All-America Football Conference") (the AFC and AAFC both merged with the NFL). and 83 players have gone in the first three rounds of the NFL draft.
